questions.question: Why is a rough capacity sheet useful before architecture?
options: It anchors component choices in scale, throughput, and cost rather than intuition alone.
It removes the need for clarifying questions.
It guarantees production-accurate numbers during the interview.
It is only helpful for storage-heavy systems.
questions.explanation: The sheet gives directional numbers that shape the design.

questions.question: Which input is most important for preventing average load from hiding real stress?
options: A peak factor or burst multiplier.
A list of preferred databases.
A count of diagram boxes.
A transcript of scope clarification.
questions.explanation: Burstiness often changes overload and scaling decisions more than the average.

questions.question: What is the right attitude toward numbers in a system design interview?
options: Use rounded estimates with stated assumptions so the numbers are auditable and decision-relevant.
Avoid numbers unless the interviewer insists on exact values.
Use as many decimal places as possible to appear rigorous.
Focus on only one metric because architecture rarely depends on multiple constraints.
questions.explanation: Interview sizing is about useful directionality and clarity, not fake precision.

questions.question: Which failure mode most often corrupts capacity reasoning?
options: Treating all requests as identical and ignoring traffic mix or payload differences.
Writing the estimates in markdown tables.
Comparing annual and daily storage separately.
Rounding values to an order of magnitude.
questions.explanation: Traffic shape and payload diversity often dominate architecture choices.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with using a compact capacity sheet instead of a full spreadsheet model?
options: It is faster and easier to communicate, but it leaves out deeper second-order cost and operational effects.
It increases detail while reducing speed.
It makes peak load impossible to discuss.
It removes the need for later observability design.
questions.explanation: Compact sheets optimize for interview utility, not full forecasting.

questions.question: Which output most directly influences edge or CDN decisions?
options: Annual storage growth only.
Peak bandwidth and payload size distribution.
The number of post-interview debrief items.
The count of database tables.
questions.explanation: Bandwidth and object size shape cache and egress strategy strongly.

questions.question: Which observability signal best shows whether your own sizing discipline is improving?
options: Whether later design reviews still uncover missing units, assumptions, or burst reasoning.
How colorful the capacity sheet template looks.
The number of total rows in the markdown table.
How many cloud products are mentioned in the architecture.
questions.explanation: Improvement appears when fewer essential sizing dimensions are missed.

questions.question: If only 1 percent of requests have very large payloads, what should you do first?
options: Ignore the outliers and size only the median request.
Separate the traffic classes so the large-payload tail can influence bandwidth and storage decisions explicitly.
Assume the system is too complex for rough math.
Replace the whole architecture with a queue.
questions.explanation: Mixed traffic classes often require separate reasoning even in a rough model.
